title: Unequal spatial patterns and differentiated city roles in the intercity telemedicine system
data_sources: China Digital Inclusive Finance Index (Peking University)

data_scope.spatial_coverage: China, city/prefecture level (cities as nodes, telemedicine flows as edges)
data_scope.consultation_records: large-scale national; exact count not stated (raw records restricted)
data_scope.processed_data: released via figshare 10.6084/m9.figshare.31872136 (network datasets + Python code)

core_conclusion: China's intercity telemedicine system forms a highly interconnected network that raises systemic efficiency but generates new inequalities by concentrating flows in a few dominant hubs. It partly breaks the traditional central-place hierarchy, reorganizing around medical expertise and patient need rather than nearest administrative center. City roles are sharply differentiated: dominant hubs centralize activity, many cities stay marginal, yet some low-resource cities use digital platforms to attract cross-provincial demand - a dual mechanism concentrating supply in top hubs while expanding outreach for ordinary cities.
why_it_matters: First national network-level characterization of intercity telemedicine; reframes the digital-divide debate by showing online healthcare re-concentrates rather than erases inequality; tests central-place theory in a platform-era setting; delivers an actionable city-role typology for telehealth policy.
why_nature: Couples digital service flows to urban spatial structure and equity; provides a mechanism (concentration vs. outreach) not just an effect; empirically confronts central-place theory with platform data; yields a policy-translatable differentiated city-role typology.
added_value_to_my_research: Paired online-offline accessibility metric -> two-channel R2SFCA splitting virtual and physical catchments
Learned graph role/community embeddings -> transferable telemedicine service-region delineation
MGWR/SHAP attribution of city roles -> explainable drivers of telehealth outreach for equity policy
Deep-gravity telemedicine flow generation -> what-if engine for digital-infrastructure scenarios
Role-based intercity referral pairing -> efficiency-vs-equity facility-allocation over city networks
